channel.title: Posts on Robert Akhmerov | v31dt
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on Robert Akhmerov | v31dt

item.title: How I Built This Portfolio
item.description:
This portfolio is built with Hugo, a static site generator. I chose Hugo because it keeps the portfolio simple, fast, and easy to maintain. Each article is written as a Markdown file, and Hugo converts those files into a static website.

The visual theme is Archie, a minimal Hugo theme. I selected it because it keeps the focus on the content: reflections, projects, and proof of professional development.

The source code is hosted in the GitHub repository v31dt/v31dt.github.io. GitHub Pages publishes the repository from the main branch. The generated Hugo output is committed in the repository root so the current GitHub Pages configuration can serve the website directly.

item.title: Cybersec Europe 2026
item.description:
Cybersec Europe 2026 took place at Brussels Expo on 20 and 21 May 2026. It is one of the largest cybersecurity events in the Benelux, with a mix of vendors, talks, technical sessions, demos, and networking opportunities. I attended it with friends from my cybersecurity circle: Artūrs Jakovļevs, Rostyslav Fedorov, Fedor Vinogradov, and Vladyslav Rovnyi.

The target audience was broad but still very relevant for me: security professionals, students, vendors, CISOs, engineers, and people working around governance, cloud, identity, data protection, and threat intelligence. That mix made the event useful because it did not feel limited to one narrow area of security. There were technical topics, but also discussions about strategy, policy, resilience, and how organisations should think about security at scale.

item.title: Future of Bug Bounty and How to Build Your Own HackBot
item.description:
For my Professional Networking podcast assignment, I spoke with Pedro Paniago, Offensive Security Manager at PwC Belgium, bug hunter, and a distinct voice in Belgium’s security scene.

Topic

The episode focuses on the future of bug bounty, AI’s influence on bug hunting, and how security researchers can build their own AI hackbot from scratch.

Guest

Pedro Paniago is an Offensive Security Manager at PwC Belgium. Besides his professional work, he is active in bug hunting and regularly shares a practical view on offensive security, automation, and the changing role of AI in cybersecurity.

item.title: PQC: How to Prepare for the Quantum Era
item.description: On 24 March 2026, I attended the Howest Tech&Meet “PQC: How to Prepare for the Quantum Era” at Howest Campus Brugge Station. The speaker was Sarah Ampe, Digital Risk Manager at EY Belgium, who works around cryptography, PKI, and post-quantum readiness. The session focused on what happens when current public-key cryptography becomes vulnerable to quantum computing, and what organisations can already do before that becomes an emergency.

item.title: DeepSeek Uncovered: The Open-Source AI Challenger from the East
item.description: On 9 December 2025, I attended the Howest Tech&Meet “DeepSeek Uncovered: The Open-Source AI Challenger from the East” at Howest Campus Brugge Station. The session was given by Dimitri Casier, lecturer in Software Development and AI at Howest. It focused on DeepSeek, why it became such a visible name in the AI discussion, and how open-source AI models compare with more closed systems from companies like OpenAI and Anthropic.

item.title: The Birth of NATO's Cyber Defence
item.description: On 2 December 2025, I attended the Howest Tech&Meet “The Birth of NATO’s Cyber Defence: Insights from an Insider” at Howest Campus Brugge Station. The speaker was Martin De Pauw, who has worked in military technology, networks, IT, and cyber within the NATO context. The talk focused on how NATO’s view of cyber changed over time, especially around 2016, when cyberspace was officially recognised as an operational domain next to land, sea, and air.

item.title: Hack The Future: AquaRadar
item.description:
Hack The Future was a good reminder that a hackathon is not only about having a good idea. It is also about knowing what you already have, and not throwing that advantage away once the pressure starts.

Hackathon

  • Name: Hack The Future
  • Challenge: Gotta Catch ‘Em All
  • Project: AquaRadar
  • Focus: a Pokemon-like fish spotting game

Reflection

The challenge was called “Gotta Catch ‘Em All”. The idea was simple and fun: build something like a Pokemon-style game, but instead of catching fictional creatures, users would spot real fish. Our version became AquaRadar, a system where someone could upload an image, get a result, review it, and slowly build up a small memory of what had already been recognised.

item.title: Threat Intelligence: from Threats to Tactics
item.description:
On 7 October 2025, I attended the Howest Tech event “Threat Intelligence: from Threats to Tactics”. The event focused on preparing organisations for modern cyber threats and translating threat knowledge into practical decisions. The session was physical, ran from 19:00 to 21:00, and fit very closely with the way I want to grow as a cybersecurity student.

Threat intelligence is one of the areas I am genuinely interested in because it is directly applicable to the work I already enjoy: bug bounty, offensive security, malware analysis, and threat-centred thinking. A lot of beginner security work starts from a checklist: test this endpoint, try this payload, scan this surface. That has value, but it can become mechanical. Threat intelligence adds context. It asks who might attack, what they usually do, what infrastructure or techniques they rely on, and which signals matter before an incident becomes obvious.
